response: The EU’s GDPR, in force since May 2018, applies to any organization handling EU residents’ personal data and enshrines rights to access, erasure, portability, explicit consent, mandatory Data Protection Officers for large-scale processing, and a 72-hour breach-reporting rule, with penalties up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million. Major enforcement actions include Meta’s €1.2 billion fine in 2023, Amazon’s €746 million sanction for ad targeting, and Google’s over €200 million in cumulative penalties. Its provisions have inspired similar laws worldwide—such as California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act—yet critics warn of heavy compliance costs for small businesses, stifled innovation, cookie-banner fatigue, and uneven member-state enforcement. Despite these challenges, GDPR has established data privacy as a fundamental right and set the global benchmark for data protection legislation.
